turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to look up a value in a HashMap using the borrowed form but my code is failing with a borrow checker error. It feels like I'm just copying strings around without actually moving them, which breaks everything.

The issue is that you're trying to use std::borrow::Borrow on something that isn't actually owned by the HashMap yet, or you're using BorrowMut when you only need a read-only view. Think of it like this: if you have a key in your HashMap but you don't own the value associated with it, you can't just grab it and do whatever you want immediately; you need to ask for permission first via the trait bounds. It's similar to how in C++ you might use references or pointers, but Rust enforces that you only get access when you've explicitly agreed to borrow it.
I see, so I should just be using AsRef instead of Borrow? But what about when I need to modify the value? Does BorrowMut handle that automatically?

Exactly, use AsRef for read-only lookups and BorrowMut if you actually intend to change the data inside the HashMap. The key distinction is that AsRef lets you treat a borrowed item as if it were owned temporarily without giving up ownership entirely, while BorrowMut requires you to hand over mutable access so the compiler knows you can mutate it safely.
Wait, but isn't this just like passing pointers in C where you manage lifetime manually? Why does Rust insist on these extra trait methods when I could just write a generic function?

That's a great question and the friction point for many developers coming from C. The reason Rust uses traits is because it needs to know at compile time whether your data can be safely borrowed, rather than relying on runtime checks or manual pointer arithmetic that can easily lead to undefined behavior. Think of it as the compiler doing the safety check instead of you having to manually track lifetimes and memory addresses.
Okay so I understand now, but what if my HashMap keys are complex types like structs? Does Borrow still apply?

Yes, Borrow applies to any type that implements the trait including your struct keys. The magic is in how the compiler infers whether you need a read or write operation based on context—if you're just looking up a value it will use AsRef automatically, but if you try to update the map entry it will switch to BorrowMut so you can safely modify it.
This makes sense now. So the borrow checker is basically asking 'are you sure this data won't be corrupted while we're using it?' and I just need to make sure my trait bounds are correct?

Exactly, that's the core idea. The borrow checker ensures thread safety and memory safety by preventing accidental re-borrowing or simultaneous mutable access. If you get errors about Borrow vs AsRef it usually means you're trying to treat something as immutable when it needs mutability or vice versa.
